coredata.dc:title: Development of carbon quantum dot-based lateral flow immunoassay for sensitive detection of aflatoxin M1 in milk
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Harpreet
Singh, Shalini
Bhardwaj, Sanjeev K.
Kaur, Gurjeet
Khatri, Madhu
Deep, Akash
Bhardwaj, Neha
coredata.dc:description: Currently, there is a great demand for simple, sensitive, and accurate sensors for aflatoxin M1 (AFM1) in dairy products. In the present research, a novel fluorescent immunosensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been developed for AFM1 analysis. The N-doped CQDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal approach using citric acid and polyethyleneimine as precursors. The CQDs showed bright blue emission under ultraviolet light irradiation and a maximum emission was observed at 450 nm upon excitation at 350 nm. The anti-AFM1 antibody (Ab) was immobilized on the as-obtained amine-functionalized CQDs and the obtained CQDs/Ab probe was then directly used for developing the immunoassays for AFM1. The fluorescence of the CQDs/Ab solution was effectively quenched in the presence of increasing AFM1 concentrations. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescent nanosensor exhibited high sensitivity towards AFM1 in the range of 0.2–0.8 ng/mL with low limit of detection i.e., 0.07 ng/mL in standard buffer. Furthermore, the CQDs/Ab immunosensor was developed as a lateral flow design for detecting the aflatoxin residues in milk. This strategy can be used for the development of low-cost, rapid, and highly sensitive sensor strips for the detection of AFM1 in dairy products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aflatoxin M1
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Fluorescence
Immunosensor
Lateral flow assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bioconjugation of anti-AFM1 antibody with CQDs
Quantification of AFM1 in aqueous solution
Synthesis and characterization of CQDs and CQDs/Ab immunosensor
Quantitative detection of AFM1 in standard buffer and milk
Lateral flow immunoassay (LFIA) for AFM1 detection in milk

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the synthesized N-doped CQDs (A) HRTEM image of N-CQDs (Inset shows the interplanar lattice spacing). (B) EDX spectra showing the elemental composition of the CQDs. (C) Optical properties of the CQDs: Absorption spectra, excitation, and emission spectra. (D) The fluorescent spectra of the CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (Inset shows the photograph of CQDs under UV light irradiation).
(A) UV–Vis and (B) FTIR spectra of synthesized CQDs and CQDs/Ab.

(A) The fluorescent spectra of the CQDs/Ab at different excitation wavelengths (B) The emission spectra of the CQDs/Ab immunosensor alone, and after incubation with 0.2 ng/mL AFM1 (λext = 350 nm). (C) Influence of pH on the fluorescent intensity of the CQDs/Ab immunosensor (λext = 350 nm, λem = 450 nm).

Fluorescent detection of AFM1 with CQDs/Ab immunosensor (A) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs/Ab in presence of different AFM1 concentrations (0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1 ng/mL prepared in 0.1 M PBS) (λext = 350 nm). (B) A curve depicting the extent of fluorescence quenching of CQDs/Ab at different concentrations of AFM1 (Inset: Linear calibration curve).

Fluorescent detection of AFM1 in milk (A) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs/Ab with different AFM1 concentrations prepared in pretreated milk samples (λext = 350 nm) (B) A curve depicting the extent of fluorescence quenching of CQDs/Ab at different concentrations of AFM1 spiked in milk samples (Inset: Linear calibration curve).

Evaluation of long-term stability and selectivity of the sensor (A) The fluorescent intensity of CQDs/Ab sensor at different time periods from day 1 to day 90 (λex = 350 nm, n = 3) (B) Change in fluorescence intensity (ΔF) of CQDs/Ab in the presence of 0.1 ng/ml AFM1, 0.1 ng/ml zearalenone (ZEN), and the mixture of AFM1 and ZEN (λex = 350 nm, n = 3).

(A) Fabrication of lateral flow assay with CQDs/Ab for detection of AFM1 (B) Fluorescence intensity responses of the paper sensor for detection of AFM1 (concentration ranging from 0.01 to 1 ng/mL) in spiked milk samples. (C) Linear calibration curve of the change in fluorescence response of CQDs/Ab in lateral flow assay strips with AFM1 spiked in milk samples.
A comparison of different studies on the sensing ability of reported optical sensors for the detection of AFM1.

head.ce:title: Development of carbon quantum dot-based lateral flow immunoassay for sensitive detection of aflatoxin M1 in milk

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, University Institute of Engineering Technology (UIET), Panjab University, Chandigarh, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, University Institute of Engineering Technology (UIET), Panjab University, Chandigarh, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR- Central Scientific Instruments Organization, Sector 30 C, Chandigarh 160030, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CSIR- Central Scientific Instruments Organization, Sector 30 C, Chandigarh 160030, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Research Material and Solutions (ARMS), Technology Business Incubator, IISER Mohali, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Research Material and Solutions (ARMS), Technology Business Incubator

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Research Material and Solutions (ARMS), Technology Business Incubator, IISER Mohali, Punjab, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, there is a great demand for simple, sensitive, and accurate sensors for aflatoxin M1 (AFM1) in dairy products. In the present research, a novel fluorescent immunosensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been developed for AFM1 analysis. The N-doped CQDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal approach using citric acid and polyethyleneimine as precursors. The CQDs showed bright blue emission under ultraviolet light irradiation and a maximum emission was observed at 450 nm upon excitation at 350 nm. The anti-AFM1 antibody (Ab) was immobilized on the as-obtained amine-functionalized CQDs and the obtained CQDs/Ab probe was then directly used for developing the immunoassays for AFM1. The fluorescence of the CQDs/Ab solution was effectively quenched in the presence of increasing AFM1 concentrations. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescent nanosensor exhibited high sensitivity towards AFM1 in the range of 0.2–0.8 ng/mL with low limit of detection i.e., 0.07 ng/mL in standard buffer. Furthermore, the CQDs/Ab immunosensor was developed as a lateral flow design for detecting the aflatoxin residues in milk. This strategy can be used for the development of low-cost, rapid, and highly sensitive sensor strips for the detection of AFM1 in dairy products.

ce:section.ce:para: Aflatoxins (AF) are a major group of mycotoxins produced as the highly toxic metabolites by fungi such as Fusarium, Aspergillus, and Penicillium (Mahato et al., 2019; Nazhand et al., 2020). Aflatoxins particularly aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), aflatoxin B2 (AFB2), and aflatoxin M1 (AFM1) are known to be carcinogenic, mutagenic, teratogenic, and they are associated with several health risks in humans (Ferreira et al., 2019; Rushing & Selim, 2019). The presence of aflatoxins in foods and dairy products is a major risk to humans. AFM1 is the hydroxylated form of AFB1 and is mainly secreted in the milk of mammals that consume AFB1 (Min et al., 2020; Nguyen et al., 2020). The consumption of AFM1 contaminated food such as milk and dairy products can lead to severe health problems including decreased immune response, increased susceptibility to infections, acute poisoning (aflatoxicosis), and reduced functioning of the liver (Afum et al., 2016; Giovati et al., 2015). The International Agency for Research on Cancer has qualified AFM1 as a group I carcinogen due to its hepatotoxicity and potential carcinogenicity. Different regulatory agencies such as European Commission and the US Food and Drug Administration have strictly regulated maximum permissible levels for AFM1in milk at 25 ng/kg and 0.5 µg/l, respectively (Omar, 2016). As a result, monitoring the presence of AFM1 in food has become critical for protecting consumers from its hazards and assuring food product quality.

The conventional methods commonly used for the detection of AFM1 include thin-layer chromatography (TLC), high-performance liquid chromatography-fluorescent detection (HPLC-FLD), liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), and liquid chromatography/atmospheric pressure chemical ionization mass spectrometry (LC/APCI-MS) (Busman et al., 2015; Mao et al., 2015). Though these methods provide accurate results, they require complex sample preparation steps, trained personnel, sophisticated and bulkier instrumentation. In addition, the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) technique is also used for food toxins detection. However, this technique is also limited by long incubation times, false-positive results, limited detection range, and low sensitivity (Pang et al., 2020; Tarannum et al., 2020). Hence, there is a need to develop novel sensing platforms that can provide highly specific and sensitive detection of AFM1 rapidly and cost-effectively.

The advances in nanomaterial science and technology have enabled the development of novel sensors with high sensitivity and detection performance. The advantageous properties of the nanomaterials, conjugated with specific biorecognition elements, have led to the development of several useful and highly sensitive optical and electrochemical sensors (Bhardwaj et al., 2019; Gupta et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020). As one of the promising and reliable alternatives, fluorescent biosensors have caught the attention of the scientific community in recent years as they can allow efficient and rapid analysis of AF. In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been identified as excellent fluorescent nanomaterials that possess rich surface chemistry, low toxicity, biocompatibility, water solubility, and good photoluminescent properties (Molaei, 2020; Nazri et al., 2021). The fluorescent assays, based on the CQDs, have been developed as a novel optical sensing technology for highly sensitive and quick detection of a range of analytes in aqueous solutions (Molaei, 2019; Nazri et al., 2021; Singh et al., 2021). Seminal contributions have been made by the researchers to develop fluorescent probes for aflatoxins utilizing the antibody or aptamer conjugated CQDs. For instance, Wang et al. have reported an ultrasensitive aptamer-based sensor for AFB1 detection. They used nitrogen-doped CQDs and assembled them on the aptamer functionalized AuNPs to achieve a good detection limit of 16 pM (Wang et al., 2016). Another research reported the development of the CQDs doped molecularly imprinted polymer for the analysis of aflatoxins B1. This work utilized a column chromatography-based method, wherein HPLC coupled fluorescent detection was explored (Liang et al., 2018). Recently, the CQDs obtained from citric acid and ethylenediamine were developed for an inner-field effect immunosensor to detect AFM1 residues in milk with a low LOD of 0.0186 ng/mL (Li et al., 2021). In addition, some lateral flow immunoassays devices have also been fabricated for aflatoxin detection in milk samples (Anfossi et al., 2013; Han et al., 2019).

Herein, the nitrogen-doped CQDs have been prepared via a facile hydrothermal method and then these were characterized using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), photoluminescence, and Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The prepared CQDs provided rich surface chemistry with abundant amine groups (–NH2 functionality) that were utilized to further link with anti-AFM1 antibody via EDC/NHS carbodiimide coupling. The performance of the above-developed immunosensor was evaluated with respect to different concentrations of AFM1 prepared in standard buffer and milk samples. Furthermore, the technique has been extended to fabricate a CQDs-based fluorescent lateral flow assay, which was subsequently used to detect aflatoxin M1 residues in milk. Compared with the previous reports, the developed method can be a more sensitive tool for on-site and direct detection of AFM1 in milk and dairy products based on fluorescence analysis.

Aflatoxin M1 from Sigma Aldrich and anti-AFM1 antibodies from My BioSource were obtained. Standard stock solutions of AFM1 (1 mg/mL) were prepared in acetonitrile and stored in amber glass vials. The chemicals such as citric acid (CA, Fisher Scientific), polyethyleneimine (PEI, Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd.), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride [EDC.HCl, SRL chemicals], N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, Thermo Fisher Scientific), phosphate buffer saline (PBS, Himedia), and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, SD Fine chemicals) were used directly as received without any further purification. Quinine sulfate (Sigma-Aldrich) was used as a reference standard for quantum yield estimation. Whatman filter paper (WhatmanTM), dialysis membrane-70 (MW cutoff 12,000, Himedia), and Acrodisc syringe filter (Pall Corporation) were also used during the experimental work. The blank strip cassette for lateral flow assay development was procured from Reliable Pro-detect Biomedicals Pvt. Ltd. Double distilled water obtained from a water distillation system (INSIF India, Pvt. Ltd.) was used throughout for preparing aqueous solutions.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy(HRTEM) images were captured using JEOL JEM 2100 Plus HRTEM, equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray analyzer (EDX). The images obtained were further processed using ImageJ software (IJ 1.46r, NIH) to assess the surface morphology and size measurements. The UV–Visible absorbance spectra between the range 200–800 nm were obtained using Cary 60 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of both solution-based and paper-based materials were collected using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer (Agilent Technologies) at room temperature. The FTIR spectra were collected with a Perkin Elmer FT-IR spectrophotometer within the wavenumber scan range of 4000 to 400 cm−1 with an accuracy of 1 cm−1.

The photoluminescent N-doped CQDs were synthesized according to a hydrothermal process (Demirci et al., 2020; Meierhofer et al., 2020). Briefly, citric acid (1 g) as the carbon source and polyethyleneimine (1 g) as the nitrogen source were dissolved completely in 25 mL of deionized water under magnetic stirring. The mixture solution was then transferred to a stainless-steel-lined Teflon reactor and treated at a temperature of 180 °C for 10 h in a hot air oven. The obtained yellow–brown solution was dispersed in 50 mL water and followed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to separate any precipitates and larger particles. The high-quality N-doped CQDs were finally obtained by dialyzing the above-collected supernatant against ultrapure water for 48 h. These CQDs were stored at 4 °C.

The quantum yield (QY) of the as-synthesized CQDs was determined by using quinine sulfate (dissolved in 0.1 M sulphuric acid) as reference (Pacquiao et al., 2018; Sahu et al., 2012). Quinine sulfate is known to possess a QY (Φ) of 54% at 340 nm excitation wavelength. Five different concentrations of each, quinine sulfate and CQDs were prepared, maintaining the absorbance values of these solutions lesser than 0.1 at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. This was done to avoid the reabsorption effects. The fluorescence spectra of the above solutions were recorded at 340 nm. The absolute values of the quantum yields were calculated by comparing the integrated PL intensity (at 340 nm) and absorbance values (at 340 nm) of the CQDs with the reference. The data was plotted and the quantum yield was calculated according to the following equation:
Φ x = Φ S T .
I x
A
ST
n
x
2
I
ST
A
x
n
ST
2
Where, Φx and ΦST are the quantum yields of CQDs (x) and quinine sulfate (standard, ST) respectively; Ix and IST are the integrated luminescent intensities; Ax and AST are absorbance values; and n is the refractive index.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioconjugation of anti-AFM1 antibody with CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs/antibody (CQDs/Ab) conjugation was achieved via a carbodiimide-mediated approach using water-soluble EDC-NHS. Briefly, 100 μL of the anti-aflatoxin antibody solution (10 μg/mL) was left to incubate for 1 h at 37 °C with 10 mM EDC and 5 mM NHS prepared in 900 μL of 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 7.4). After incubation, the above mixture was added into 10 mL of the purified CQDs solution to allow the formation of the antibody-immobilized immunosensor. The contents were further incubated overnight at 4 °C to deactivate the remaining EDC-NHS. The CQDs/Ab immunosensor was purified by dialyzing it against ultrapure water for 24 h, before storing at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of AFM1 in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para:
For the quantitative measurement of AFM1, the fluorescence response of the CQDs/Ab sensor against different concentrations of AFM1 (0.01 to 1 ng/mL) prepared in 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 7.4) was measured under optimal experimental conditions. An optimized volume of 900 µL of CQDs/Ab solution was mixed with 100 µL of different AFM1 concentrations at room temperature (RT, 25 ± 2 °C) to make a final volume to 1 mL. After gentle mixing and incubating the mixtures for 30 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The fluorescent intensity of the CQDs/Ab solution was referred to as F0 whereas that of the CQDs/Ab mixed with AFM1 solution was denoted as F. All the emission spectra (emission wavelength of 450 nm) were taken at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm at RT. The experimental data were fitted to determine the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) using the following equations:
LOD = 3.3 ∗
S b
LOQ = 10 ∗
S b
where S represents the standard deviation of the blank signal and b refers to the slope of the calibration curve obtained by plotting ΔF (=F0-F) vs log of AFM1 concentration.

The impact of pH on the fluorescent intensity of CQDs/Ab sensor solution was also measured. For this, a specific volume of the CQDs/Ab solution dispersed in double distilled water was set to pH values from pH 2–11 by using 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH. For optimizing the incubation time, the fluorescent response of the CQDs/Ab against a fixed concentration of AFM1 was checked for different time intervals (0–60 min) and data was recorded till the response stabilized.

The sample of Pasteurized milk was collected from a local market in Chandigarh, India, and then pre-treated according to a standard protocol (Zhou et al., 2019). In this pretreatment process, a 2 mL milk sample was first diluted with 8 mL of 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 7.4) and then centrifuged for 10 min at 8000 rpm (at RT) to remove the fat layers. The supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22 µm disposable syringe filter to obtain a clear solution. The obtained filtrate was spiked with different concentrations of AFM1 and further analyzed with the prepared CQDs/Ab immunosensor (excitation wavelength of 350 nm).

The long-duration stability of the immunosensor’s fluorescent intensity was checked from day 1 to day 90. Additionally, other microbial toxins were also tested as the possible interference substances to evaluate the selectivity of the immunosensor. The fluorescent measurements were recorded as mentioned above in section 2.5.

The lateral flow immunoassay strip comprised of a sample pad, test zone (T), and control zone (C) inserted into the rigid plastic cassettes. The sample pad was pre-treated with a phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4) solution and then left to dry at 37 °C. The CQDs/Ab probe was immobilized to the nitrocellulose paper strip using PVA solution to form control and test zone, respectively. PVA (1 g) was mixed in 20 mL distilled water and heated till its complete solubilization to form a clear transparent solution. The solution was then allowed to cool to RT. Next, 5 mL of the prepared PVA solution was mixed with 2 mL of CQDs/Ab solution and stirred. This mixture solution was then gently dispensed on the separate nitrocellulose membrane strips (dimensions 5 mm), dried, and then pasted over a blank assay strip to trace as the control line and test line. The distance between the C and T lines was set to 3 mm and sealed with adhesive tape to avoid intermixing. For analysis of AFM1, 1 µL of different concentrations of AFM1 prepared in milk extracts was dispensed onto the sample pad that moved upwards through capillary action to the test zone and bonded to the probe. The strips were dried for 30 min at RT. Further, the fluorescence intensity of the T and C zones at the strip was measured using the solid-state photoluminescence assembly for quantitative toxin analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs and CQDs/Ab immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The N-doped CQDs were obtained by a one-step hydrothermal treatment of citric acid (CA) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. Since both CA and PEI consist of multiple functional groups, the CQDs are formed from a large network of both precursor molecules to achieve a covalent linkage between PEI (amine groups) and CA (carboxylic groups) (Hou et al., 2017). The general structure and surface morphology of the synthesized CQDs were characterized using HRTEM analysis (Fig. 1 A). A quasi-spherical structure of CQDs was observed. Based on the analysis of the mean diameter of 20 N-doped CQDs particles by ImageJ software, they are estimated to have an average diameter of 8 nm. Relatively large particle size of the CQDs indicates their successful modification by PEI. The HRTEM analysis also showed high-density growth of CQDs with uniform sizes, while some agglomeration was observed. A distinct interplanar lattice d-spacing of 0.2 nm is also evident which is similar to interplanar distance reported in the case of previously synthesized carbon dots (Kumar et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2013). Fig. 1B represents the elemental composition of the CQDs analyzed with the energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX). The sample contained mainly carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen thereby confirming the synthesis of pure nitrogen-doped CQDs without any impurity.

The spectroscopic characterizations of CQDs and CQDs/Ab immunosensor were performed with UV–visible absorption and photoluminescence spectra. Fig. 1C represents the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the synthesized N-doped CQDs exhibiting a typical peak at 350 nm due to n → π* transition of some functional groups including the CO bond. In addition, a specific peak at 235 nm was related to π → π* transitions of the aromatic domains in the carbonaceous core of the CQDs, as also reported elsewhere (Lu et al., 2016). In the UV–Vis spectra of the CQDs/Ab immunosensor (Fig. 2 A), no distinct peak was observed at 350 nm probably as a result of the conjugation of anti-AFM1 antibody onto the surface of the N-doped CQDs.

The N-doped CQDs were transparent under the natural light conditions; however, they showed bright blue color under the UV light irradiation. This observation confirmed the highly fluorescent nature of the synthesized CQDs. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the N-doped CQDs were also recorded at different excitation wavelengths to characterize the fluorescent emission performance (Fig. 1D). The N-doped CQDs showed the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescent behavior. The emission peak at 310 nm excitation was of the lowest intensity, which increased for larger wavelengths until 350 nm. Beyond 350 nm excitation (e.g., at 370 nm), there was a decrease in the PL intensity. Hence, the optimal excitation wavelength for the N-doped CQDs was identified as 350 nm. The corresponding emission peak was observed at a wavelength of 450 nm. For all other PL studies, the excitation wavelength was maintained at 350 nm. The CQDs were known to show good photostability under continuous excitation at 350 nm. The surface passivation of the CQDs with PEI resulted in their decoration with amine groups (–NH2) which led to the increased fluorescent intensity of the N-doped CQDs (Liu et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2021). The QY of the N-doped CQDs was calculated as 22% with respect to quinine sulfate as the reference, as calculated from the graph (Supplementary information).

FTIR spectra of the N-doped CQDs and CQDs/Ab sensor (Fig. 2B) were recorded to identify the chemical functional groups (Wang et al., 2015). The N-doped CQDs showed the absorption bands at 3338 and 1637 cm−1 associated with the stretching and bending vibrations of OH and NH bond indicating the presence of surface hydroxyl and amine (NH2) functional groups. Also, a broad absorption band at 3337 cm−1 reduced to a narrower one at 3306 cm−1 after the attachment of antibodies over the CQDs. This observation suggests the formation of amide bonds between the carboxyl moieties of the anti-AFM1 antibody (activated by EDC/NHS coupling) and the amine groups of the CQDs. A band at 1563 cm−1 corresponds to the amide bond, confirming the amidation reaction between the CQDs and the AFM1 antibody. Furthermore, the bands at 1710 and 1213 cm−1 can be ascribed to the CO and CN stretching vibrations, respectively present in the amide linkage. The FTIR data therefore successfully confirmed the bioconjugation of anti-AFM1 antibody on the amine-functionalized CQDs.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CQDs/Ab immunosensor were also recorded (Fig. 3 A) at different excitation wavelengths to characterize the fluorescent emission performance The fluorescent intensity of the CQDs/Ab solution was slightly lesser than that of the CQDs alone at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. This reduction in the fluorescence intensity can be related to the introduction of antibody molecules over the surface of CQDs, which absorbed some fraction of the excitation energy. The addition of a random concentration of AFM1 (e.g., 1 ng/mL, in PBS buffer) into the CQDs/Ab probe again caused a noticeable fluorescence quenching as shown in Fig. 3B. The optical response has been utilized to develop a CQDs/Ab sensor for AFM1.

The quantification of the antibody present in CQDs/Ab conjugates was measured by circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy (Li et al., 2015). Considering that the presence of CQDs did not interfere with the CD signal of antibodies in the range from 250 to 200 nm, the CD spectra of different concentrations of antibody (0.025–0.2 µg/mL) and antibody in presence of CQDs was measured. After that, the CD spectra of the antibody (CQDs/Ab) were measured in the presence of CQDs. The amount of antibody bound in CQDs/Ab immunoconjugates was measured from the calibration curve obtained from the CD spectra of known antibody concentrations. It was observed that the amount of antibody found in the CQDs/Ab solution was around 0.065 µg/mL. The detailed information is provided in the supplementary information.

For optimization, the fluorescence spectra of the CQDs/Ab probe were measured in the presence of different concentrations of AFM1 with respect to parameters like assay buffer pH, incubation time, etc. The studies on the effect of pH (from pH 3 to 10) on the properties of the CQDs/Ab solution revealed a maximum fluorescent intensity at around pH 6–7 (Fig. 3C). The fluorescent intensity showed some decline under both too acidic and alkaline conditions. Therefore, the prepared nanosensor was better suited to operate in conditions from around 6–7.

Next, the response time of the sensor probe was optimized by incubating the 900 µL of the CQDs/Ab solution with 100 µL of the AFM1 (0.01–1 ng/mL, pH 7.4) at RT for varying time intervals (0 to 50 min). The mixture solution was mixed gently with the help of a vortex and further evaluated for fluorescence intensity. It was observed that the fluorescent response took about 30 min for stabilization and there was no further change beyond that. Therefore, the response time of the present sensor is claimed to be 30 min. All the other sensing experiments for the development of an assay for AFM1 were conducted under neutral pH conditions keeping an incubation time of 30 min and using 0.1 M PBS buffer solution as the aqueous media (pH 7.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of AFM1 in standard buffer and milk

ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the fluorescence-based immunosensing was tested toward the spiked milk samples (added AFM1 = 0.01–1 ng/mL). As mentioned in the preceding section, the fluorescence spectra and calibration curve were obtained for the milk samples (Fig. 5 ). The detection limit of the sensor, in this case, was found to be 0.08 ng/mL. It was concluded that a slightly higher LOD than the buffer samples was related to the presence of interferents in the milk sample. AFM1is a relatively small molecule (328.3 g mol−1) which exhibits slight affinity towards milk proteins (such as casein, etc.) and can bind to them. In milk, it is partitioned into the water and cream parts of milk. Considering the semipolar characteristics of AFM1, a strong relationship between casein and the toxin may be expected. In fact, appraisals have estimated that 30% of AFM1is associated with milk non-fat solids. Milk processing usually has a dramatic effect on AFM1concentration (Granados-Chinchilla, 2016). There can also be possibility of interactions between AFM1and other dairy components, including whey proteins. Nonetheless, the sensor could still detect below the most stringent maximum tolerable limit value of 0.5 µg/l (0.5 ng/mL) in milk as recommended by the US Food and Drug Administration (USFDA) (Turna & Wu, 2021). Thus, the developed fluorescent immunosensor can facilitate the detection of AFM1 in dairy products and can become an important tool for food quality control analysis.

Fig. 6 A depicts the effect of storage time on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs/Ab probe. The probe maintained its integrity and no significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity was observed even after 90 days. There was no change in the emission wavelength also. This finding clearly suggests that the fluorescence of the CQDs/Ab solution was stable and this probe can be preserved for prolonged durations. To investigate the selectivity of the CQDs/Ab immunosensor, its fluorescence response was also recorded against zearalenone (0.1 ng/mL) and a mixture of AFM1 and zearalenone (0.1 ng/mL each). As shown in Fig. 6B, the CQDs/Ab probe was unresponsive to the non-specific zearalenone analyte. The response of the sensor in the co-presence of AFM1 and zearalenone matched the response of AFM1 only. These studies have implied that the prepared sensing platform exhibits great potential for highly selective detection of AFM1 in several samples, such as milk and dairy products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lateral flow immunoassay (LFIA) for AFM1 detection in milk
ce:section.ce:para: Lateral flow assays are considered as the simple and portable sensing platform for the on-site detection of analytes. Conventional lateral flow assays are generally based on sandwich and competitive formats. Generally, the strips used for LFA contain four different parts: sample application pad, conjugate pad, nitrocellulose membrane, and adsorbent pad. The lateral flow strip used in this study contains the nitrocellulose membrane immobilized with CQDs/Ab probe as test (T) and control (C) lines respectively and the area between the two lines (3 mm) was sealed using an adhesive tape so that no reagent flows between the two lines. Finally, the nitrocellulose membrane was kept at room temperature for drying. These lines gave a bright blue fluorescence when illuminated in darkness using UV light (365 nm) as shown in Fig. 7 A. The strips were then placed in plastic cassette body for LFIA development.

In this case, optimization of LFIA involves studies in which the concentrations of antibodies are varied to prepare CQDs conjugated with different amounts of antibodies (Anfossi et al., 2013; Han et al., 2019). The effect of antibody concentration on LFIA sensitivity was then investigated. The results indicated that the antibody amount for conjugation can affect the antibody density on CQDs surface which in turn, would influence the avidity of the prepared antibody-CQDs conjugate and thereby can alter the LFIA sensitivity. Therefore, a simple test was used to optimize this parameter aiming to obtain the best LFA sensitivities. It was observed that with the increase of antibody conc. up to 0.05 µg/ml, the prepared CQDs/Ab conjugate showed high fluorescent intensity on T line but did not show significant change in fluorescence when incubated with AFM1 (Table S1). In other words, the immunosensor was not working at low antibody concentrations. However, at the antibody concentration of 0.1 µg/ml and above, the immunosensor showed a change in fluorescence on addition of AFM1. However, the use of high Ab concentration may lead to decrease in fluorescence intensity of CQD probes. Thus, the optimum antibody concentration of 0.1 µg/ml was utilized for conjugation with CQDs for development of CQD/Ab probes on LFIA (Supplementary information). Next, six different concentrations of AFM1 were spiked in the milk extracts and then introduced onto the sample pads. As the analyte moved along the strip, the AFM1 content formed a complex with the antibody attached CQDs. The fluorescence at the test line (F) and control line (F0) was then measured by a solid-state photoluminescence accessory. The quantitative changes could be detected by taking the fluorescence spectra of the test lines at different concentrations of AFM1. Accordingly, a calibration curve was created by plotting the change in fluorescent intensity (F0-F) of the sensor against the concentration of AFM1 in milk samples. As a result, the linear detection range of the assay was found to be 0.2–0.8 ng/ml of AFM1 with good correlation. As shown in Fig. 7B, higher concentrations of the spiked analyte in the milk sample induced more fluorescence quenching. Also, a significant observation was found that there was a shift in the emission range of the solution with increasing concentrations of the AFM1 in milk samples. This was mainly due to the exhibition of strong fluorescence of AFM1 under UV conditions as the emission fluorescence peak of aflatoxin has been reported to be located between 418 and 440 nm under a 350–370-nm excitation source (Hruska et al., 2014; Manetta et al., 2005). As described above, the proposed prototype lateral flow assays can prove to be highly useful and portable detection tools for the detection of aflatoxins in various matrices.

The present study has highlighted the application of a fluorescent immunosensor developed by bioconjugation of anti-AFM1 antibody with N-doped CQDs for highly efficient detection of AFM1. The advantages of CQDs to be chosen as the optical sensing materials for immunoassay development are their highly photoluminescent properties, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and good stability. The interactions between the CQDs/Ab immunosensor and AFM1 resulted in a decrease in fluorescent intensity of the CQDs/Ab complex. This method has reliable sensitivity, selectivity, and stability for detection of AFM1 in approx. 30 min within the range of 0.2–0.8 ng/mL and LOD of 0.07 ng/mL. Also, the sensor could achieve AFM1 detection in spiked milk samples. Furthermore, the synthesized probes could be immobilized on paper strips for the development of lateral flow assays that could efficiently detect AFM1 in milk samples. The present work promises an effective means to develop a sensitive, selective, and portable nanosensor for the detection of AFM1 in food products. The developed sensor prototype can also be utilized for sensing a series of other toxins present in food products in a rapid manner.

Harpreet Singh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Shalini Singh: Methodology. Sanjeev K. Bhardwaj: Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Gurjeet Kaur: Methodology. Madhu Khatri: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision. Akash Deep: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Validation, Formal analysis, Supervision. Neha Bhardwaj: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Neha Bhardwaj acknowledges DST (Department of Science and Technology) for the INSPIRE FACULTY grant (Reg. no. IFA18-LSPA 127). Madhu Khatri acknowledges DBT Builder grant(BT/INF/22/SP41295/2020)at Panjab University Chandigarh. The authors acknowledge Central Instrumentation Facility and Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation Facility at Panjab University Chandigarh for providing instrumentation for characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.133374.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
